title: Word2Vec: The Core Intuition
concept: Word2Vec learns word meanings by looking at their neighbors; words that appear in similar contexts should end up with similar embedding vectors.

exercises.prompt: Since computers don't speak human languages, how do they store and compare words?

options.text: By using math and numbers called vectors.
By converting every word into an image.

exercises.explain: Computers don't have human brains, so they can't 'understand' concepts directly. Instead, they use math (vectors) to represent relationships between words.

cards.title: Do Computers Read Minds?
cards.body: How does a computer understand that 'cat' and 'kitten' are related, but 'cat' and 'microwave' are completely different? It doesn't use a dictionary. Instead, it plays a game of looking at neighbor words! Let's find out how.

cards.title: What is Word2Vec?
cards.body: Word2Vec is a smart AI technique that represents words as list of numbers (vectors). Instead of reading a dictionary, it learns the meaning of a word by analyzing its 'neighbors'—the words that frequently appear right next to it in sentences.

cards.title: The Party Guest Rule
cards.body: Imagine a party where you don't know anyone. If you notice that both Leo and Sam hang out near the snack table, chat with the dog, and love drinking apple juice, you'll guess they share similar personalities. Word2Vec does the exact same thing! It doesn't look inside a word; it looks at who the word hangs out with.

cards.title: Context in Action
cards.body:
Think about these sentences:
1. "The cute *puppy* barked in the yard."
2. "The cute *doggy* barked in the yard."

Because "puppy" and "doggy" both hang out next to "cute" and "barked," Word2Vec figures out they must have very similar meanings and gives them similar vectors!

cards.title: The Definition Myth
cards.body: Many people think Word2Vec understands the real-world definition of words, like knowing that a banana is a yellow fruit you can peel. In reality, it only knows that "banana" often appears next to "eat", "yellow", and "smoothie". It's all about context, not real-world experience!

cards.title: The Word Map
cards.body: Word2Vec positions words in a mathematical space based on how they are used. Words with similar contexts end up close together.

left.text: 'Coffee' and 'Tea' are placed close together because they share neighbors like 'hot', 'cup', and 'drink'.

right.text: 'Tractor' is placed far away because it hangs out with words like 'farm', 'soil', and 'pull'.

exercises.prompt: If the words "coffee" and "tea" constantly appear next to the words "drink", "hot", and "mug", what will Word2Vec do to their vectors?

options.text: They will have very similar vector representations.
They will have completely opposite vectors.
They will have no vectors at all.

exercises.explain: Word2Vec assumes that words appearing in similar situations (contexts) share similar meanings. Therefore, their mathematical representations (vectors) are placed close to each other.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Word2Vec will group the words "cat" and "car" closely together because they both start with the letters 'c-a'.
exercises.explain: Word2Vec looks at context, not spelling. "Cat" and "car" have very different contexts, so they will not be close in Word2Vec.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the core idea of Word2Vec:
exercises.sentence: Word2Vec learns the meaning of a word by looking at its surrounding [blank].
exercises.explain: Word2Vec relies entirely on context. It looks at the words surrounding a target word to figure out its meaning.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations of how Word2Vec pairs "laptop" and "computer" makes the most sense?

options.text: It uses high-dimensional geometric hyperplanes to cross-reference syntactic structures.
It draws a digital map of the world and places word flags where they belong physically.
It checks the words surrounding 'laptop' and 'computer' and groups them together because their neighbors are alike.

exercises.explain: The first option is generated by an AI assistant trying to use overly technical and irrelevant jargon. The second option is also AI-generated, framing it as a physical map. The third option is the correct human-written explanation focusing on Word2Vec's core neighbor-based intuition.

exercises.prompt: You want to explain Word2Vec to a friend who thinks AI is magic. What is the actual 'secret sauce' behind how Word2Vec learns that two words are similar?

options.text: It reads a dictionary to look up what words mean.
It measures how many letters two words share in common.
It groups words closer together if they share similar neighbor words.
It translates words into different languages to find matches.

exercises.explain: Word2Vec does not understand definitions, grammar, or human concepts out of the box. It only looks at context windows (neighboring words) and updates vector coordinates so that words sharing similar neighbors end up close to each other in vector space.
